stewart_read: Alan's 'Yes' at 01:10 — in response to 'additional panels or a backup battery' — signals possible interest in Ion's products beyond the broken system. However, the transcript context makes this ambiguous: Alan's follow-up 'Backer battery?' at 01:24 reads more plausibly as a confused clarifying question than a firm confirmation. The signal is real but tentative — Alan may have been tracking the battery option before his primary concern (broken panels) surfaced. Strategic read: the rep could have re-anchored to this possible battery interest after the hard DQ landed, rather than exiting. Reactive read: the rep treated the DQ as a full stop and never returned to the signal.
alternative_hypothesis: Alan's 'Yes' was a reflexive acknowledgment of the question rather than genuine interest in either option — the 'Backer battery?' confusion at 01:24 supports this reading.
coaching_implication: Show the rep this moment in the 1-on-1. The question is: after the hard DQ on the broken panels, did Alan's possible battery interest create a path to a new Powerwall lead qualification? The rep never tested it. Coach the pivot: 'I can't fix what SolarCity installed — but if you're still curious about battery backup, that's actually something we can do as a standalone. Want me to have someone take a look at that separately?'

quote: you'd have to contact SolarCity, and they they would be able to help you with that.
stewart_read: The rep correctly identifies Ion cannot service the SolarCity system, but delivers the redirect without any acknowledgment of Alan's situation — he has non-functioning solar panels, which is a real inconvenience. The offer that follows ('if you were wanting to get a backup battery or additional panels, I can help you with that') lands on unacknowledged frustration and reads as a pivot-to-pitch rather than a genuine attempt to help. General acknowledgment-before-redirect is implied by good practice but is not explicitly codified in the current schema for this scenario.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have been moving quickly to preserve Alan's time, reading the short responses as a signal to be efficient rather than expansive.
coaching_implication: Coach a one-sentence acknowledgment before the redirect: something like 'That's frustrating — you've got panels that aren't doing their job.' Then bridge. The offer that follows will land differently. This is not a vulnerability disclosure scenario per the schema — it's a general empathy-before-redirect gap that applies any time the rep is delivering news the customer didn't want to hear.

quote: I can help you with getting that.
stewart_read: After the hard DQ on the broken SolarCity system, the rep re-offers additional panels and backup battery — Ion's available products. This is the right instinct. But the offer is made without re-anchoring to Alan's possible battery interest from 01:10, without acknowledging his primary frustration (broken panels), and without framing the Powerwall as a standalone solution that doesn't depend on fixing the SolarCity system. The offer floats without a hook and Alan declines. Strategic read: the rep identified the cross-sell path correctly. Reactive read: the rep failed to execute it with enough specificity or acknowledgment to make it land.
alternative_hypothesis: Alan's 'No. Thank you.' may have been inevitable regardless of framing — his primary goal was fixing the broken system, and once that was off the table, he had no remaining interest.
coaching_implication: The Powerwall is Ion's product and does not require the SolarCity system to be functional. Coach the rep to separate the two: 'The SolarCity panels — that's their system to fix. But a Tesla Powerwall is something we install independently. If you've been thinking about battery backup, that's actually a clean path we can look at on its own.' This reframe gives Alan a reason to stay on the line.

quote: who who did you get set up with?
stewart_read: This is a procedurally correct orphaned_flow step — the rep asks which company installed the existing system, which surfaces the SolarCity hard DQ. The hesitation ('who who') suggests the rep is improvising rather than executing a prepared protocol, but the question itself is right. Worth surfacing as a positive moment in the coaching session to give the manager a balanced picture.
coaching_implication: Acknowledge this as a correct move in the 1-on-1. The rep identified the orphaned scenario via Google Maps, asked the right qualifying question, and correctly applied the hard DQ. The coaching gap is what happens AFTER the DQ — not the DQ identification itself. Separating these two moments helps the rep understand what they did well and where the specific miss occurred.

quote: No. It's just the panel is not working now.
stewart_read: Alan's clarification reveals the rep's Powerwall assumption was wrong — Alan wasn't shopping for an upgrade, he has a broken system. The rep's 'Oh, okay' at 01:52 is an appropriate acknowledgment of the correction, but the rep then pivots immediately to the company question without pausing to understand the full situation. The orphaned_flow protocol asks whether the system is working — the rep gets there, but reactively rather than through a prepared sequence.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have been following an informal version of the orphaned_flow protocol from memory, which is why the sequence feels improvised but lands on the right questions.
coaching_implication: Use this moment to introduce the orphaned_flow protocol explicitly. The rep handled it adequately but without structure. A prepared sequence — own vs. lease, which company, system size, is it working — would have surfaced the hard DQ faster and left more time for the Powerwall pivot attempt.
